1: Chapter 14 introduces one of Jeremiah's most distinctive literary forms: the liturgy of drought, a communal lament structured around the people's cry, YHWH's response, and a second round of intercession. The superscription identifies the occasion: the word of YHWH that came to Jeremiah concerning the drought (devarim asher hayu el-yirmeyahu al-divrei habatzorot — literally, the matters of the droughts, plural). Drought in the ancient Near East was not merely meteorological disaster; it was a theological signal. Deuteronomy 28:22–24 listed drought and scorching heat as covenant curses for disobedience. The land's failure to rain is the land's testimony against the people.
2: Judah mourns and her gates languish; her people lament on the ground, and the cry of Jerusalem goes up. The corporate nature of the disaster is established immediately: Judah (the nation), her gates (the economic and legal centers where commerce and judgment occurred), her people, Jerusalem — the entire social structure is afflicted. The gates languishing (amlelu) is a vivid image: the places of civic life have fallen silent, their normal activity suspended by crisis. The cry going up (tza'aqat yerushalayim) echoes Exodus 2:23 — the enslaved Israelites' cry that reached YHWH. The question implicit in this echo is whether YHWH will respond again.
3: Their nobles send their servants for water; they come to the cisterns; they find no water; they return with their vessels empty. The social hierarchy is leveled by drought: even the adirim (nobles, great ones) cannot obtain water through their superior social position. Their servants return with empty jars. They are ashamed and confounded and cover their heads: the gesture of covering the head is a sign of mourning and shame (2 Samuel 15:30; Esther 6:12). The public humiliation of the powerful underscores the completeness of the disaster — drought does not respect social rank.
4: Because of the ground that is dismayed, since there is no rain on the land, the farmers are ashamed; they cover their heads. The shame extends down the social ladder from nobles to farmers. The ground itself is chatat (dismayed, shattered) — the same word used of human emotional devastation applied to the parched soil. Biblical ecology makes the land's suffering a direct reflection of the covenant community's condition. The farmers who work the ground share in its humiliation: without rain their labor is worthless.
5: Even the doe in the field forsakes her newborn fawn because there is no grass. The breakdown of instinctive maternal behavior is one of the most powerful images in the passage. The doe is not abandoning her young from cruelty but from necessity — there is nothing to sustain her milk production. When even animals cannot maintain the most basic instinct of parental care, the ecological crisis has reached its extreme. The image anticipates Lamentations 4:3–4, which uses the same motif for the siege of Jerusalem.
6: The wild donkeys stand on the bare heights; they pant for air like jackals; their eyes fail because there is no vegetation. Wild donkeys inhabited the desert fringes; during normal years they survived on sparse scrubland vegetation. Now even they suffer. The panting for air like jackals is an image of desperate respiratory distress — animals gasping in dry heat. Their eyes fail (kalu eineihem) describes the glazed, failing vision of animals dying from dehydration. The entire created order — noble, farmer, domestic animal, wild animal — suffers together.
7: Though our iniquities testify against us, act, O YHWH, for your name's sake; for our backslidings are many; we have sinned against you. The communal lament reaches its theological core: the people acknowledge that their iniquities have caused the drought. The confession is not merely formulaic; it is structurally necessary in the covenant framework. But the petition is grounded not in the people's merit (they explicitly acknowledge they have none) but in your name's sake (lema'an shemekha) — YHWH's own reputation, character, and covenantal commitment. Acting for his name's sake is acting consistently with who he has declared himself to be.
8: O you hope of Israel, its savior in time of trouble, why should you be like a stranger in the land, like a traveler who turns aside to tarry for a night? The lament form employs questions to press YHWH toward action. Hope of Israel (mikveh yisrael) — the same title Jeremiah will use in Jeremiah 17:13 — is a covenant claim: YHWH is Israel's reservoir of hope, the one they collect toward. A stranger and a traveler stopping for a night have no stake in the land — they observe suffering without commitment. The implied charge: YHWH appears uninvolved, indifferent, passing through rather than dwelling with his people.
9: Why should you be like a man confused, like a mighty warrior who cannot save? Yet you, O YHWH, are in the midst of us, and we are called by your name; do not leave us. The audacious image of YHWH as a confused warrior (gibor lo-yukhal lehoshi'a) is a rhetorical provocation — the community's complaint presses toward the edge of theological propriety. But it is ultimately reversed: you are in the midst of us, and we are called by your name. The appeal is to YHWH's indwelling presence (Temple theology) and name-claim (covenant relationship). These two facts — presence and name — are the bases for the final plea: do not leave us.
10: Thus says YHWH concerning this people: They have loved to wander; they have not restrained their feet; therefore YHWH does not accept them; now he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins. YHWH's response to the communal lament is stark refusal. The people's confession in v7 acknowledged backslidings; YHWH confirms them: they have loved to wander — the wandering is not accidental but volitional, habitual, beloved. The result is that the normal covenant mechanism of prayer-and-response does not function: YHWH does not accept them. The drought-lament is legitimate in form but cannot be effective given the state of the relationship.
11: YHWH said to me: Do not pray for the welfare of this people. The intercession-prohibition appears again (cf. Jeremiah 7:16; Jeremiah 11:14). The repetition of this prohibition is itself theologically significant: Jeremiah keeps wanting to intercede; YHWH keeps forbidding it. The prophet's instinct toward covenant solidarity with his people is genuine — but YHWH's judgment has passed the point where intercession can reverse it. Moses interceded successfully at the golden calf (Exodus 32:11–14); Samuel interceded for Israel (1 Samuel 12:23). But even those intercessors could not permanently forestall the consequences of persistent covenant violation (cf. Jeremiah 15:1).
12: Though they fast, I will not hear their cry, and though they offer burnt offering and grain offering, I will not accept them. But I will consume them by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence. The three instruments of covenant judgment — sword, famine, pestilence — form the triad that runs throughout Jeremiah (cf. Jeremiah 21:9; Jeremiah 24:10; Jeremiah 27:8; Jeremiah 29:17–18). Each represents a different vector of the curse sequence in Deuteronomy 28: military defeat (sword), agricultural failure (famine), epidemic disease (pestilence). The rejection of fasting and sacrifice as intercessory tools echoes Amos 5:21–24 and Isaiah 1:11–15: ritual performance cannot substitute for covenantal fidelity.
13: Then I said: Ah, Lord YHWH, behold, the prophets say to them, 'You shall not see the sword, nor shall you have famine, but I will give you assured peace in this place'. Jeremiah raises what seems like a mitigating factor: the false prophets are deceiving the people. If the people believe prophets who promise peace, can they be fully culpable? The defense is sincere but insufficient. Jeremiah is not excusing the people but pressing YHWH on the theological problem: the people are receiving contradictory messages — Jeremiah announces judgment, the other prophets announce peace. Who should the people believe?
14: And YHWH said to me: The prophets are prophesying lies in my name. I did not send them, nor did I command them or speak to them. They are prophesying to you a lying vision, worthless divination, and the deceit of their own minds. YHWH's answer clears away the potential defense: the false prophets are not licensed interpreters of ambiguous divine signals but frauds speaking from their own imagination. I did not send them (lo shalachtiim) is the central criterion of prophetic authority in Deuteronomy 18:20 and throughout Jeremiah (Jeremiah 23:21; Jeremiah 27:15; Jeremiah 28:15; Jeremiah 29:9). The false prophet's crime is not merely error but deliberate misrepresentation of YHWH.
15: Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the prophets who prophesy in my name although I did not send them, and who say, 'Sword and famine shall not come upon this land': By sword and famine those prophets shall be consumed. Covenant logic applies to the false prophets with precision: they promise freedom from sword and famine; they will die by sword and famine. The punishment mirrors and refutes the false message. Deuteronomy 18:20 mandated death for prophets who spoke presumptuously in YHWH's name. The covenant legal system is being enforced.
16: And the people to whom they prophesy shall be cast out in the streets of Jerusalem, victims of famine and sword, with none to bury them — them, their wives, their sons, and their daughters. The false prophets and their audiences share the same fate. The absence of burial is one of the most severe of covenant curses — Deuteronomy 28:26 threatened that bodies would be food for birds and beasts, lying unburied. Burial was a fundamental duty of family and community honor in the ancient Near East; its deprivation represents the total breakdown of social fabric.
17: You shall say to them this word: 'Let my eyes run down with tears night and day, and let them not cease, for the virgin daughter of my people is shattered with a great wound, with a very grievous blow'. Jeremiah is commanded to weep publicly — the tears are not optional personal grief but a prophetically mandated expression. The virgin daughter of my people (betulat bat-ammi) is a standard personification for the nation in its vulnerability and innocence — a people who had a future, now shattered. The wound (shever gadol, great breaking) and blow (makah nachalah, very severe blow) convey the catastrophic magnitude of the coming disaster.
18: If I go out into the field, behold, those pierced by the sword! And if I enter the city, behold, the diseases of famine! For both prophet and priest ply their trade through the land and have no knowledge. A vision of the aftermath: the landscape outside the city is littered with battlefield corpses; the city interior is full of famine victims. The double horror (sword and famine, the judgment triad's two components) is everywhere. Prophet and priest ply their trade through the land and have no knowledge: the religious establishment continues its professional religious activity while the disaster unfolds around them — a devastating critique of institutionalized religion's capacity for moral blindness.
19: Have you utterly rejected Judah? Does your soul loathe Zion? Why have you struck us down so that there is no healing for us? We looked for peace, but no good came; for a time of healing, but behold, terror. A second communal lament resumes the liturgical pattern. The questions are more desperate than the first: Have you utterly rejected (ha-ma'os ma'asta — the infinitive absolute intensifies) — have you completely finished with us? Does your soul loathe Zion? The psychological image of divine disgust presses further than any earlier lament. The question of whether YHWH has crossed the point of no return is asked directly.
20: We acknowledge our wickedness, O YHWH, and the iniquity of our fathers, for we have sinned against you. The second confession is fuller than the first (v7): it acknowledges not only present sin but the iniquity of our fathers — the generational accumulation of covenant violation that the present generation has inherited and continued. This intergenerational confession mirrors Leviticus 26:40–41, which describes the restoration condition as confessing both their own sin and their fathers' transgression. The people are using the language of restoration, pressing toward the covenant's own mercy provisions.
21: Do not spurn us, for your name's sake; do not dishonor your glorious throne; remember and do not break your covenant with us. Three imperatives structure the plea: do not spurn, do not dishonor, remember. Each invokes a different aspect of YHWH's own self-commitment: your name's sake (his declared character), your glorious throne (his Zion dwelling-presence), your covenant (his binding commitment to Israel). The logic is: breaking the covenant would be an act of self-contradiction for YHWH. The people are not arguing from their own merit but from YHWH's obligations to himself.
22: Are there any among the false gods of the nations that can bring rain? Or can the heavens give showers? Are you not he, O YHWH our God? We will hope in you, for you have made all these things. The lament ends with a doxological affirmation of YHWH as the sole source of rain and fertility — a direct polemic against Baal, the Canaanite storm-and-rain deity. The rhetorical questions (are there any gods that can bring rain?) expect the answer no. The conclusion: only YHWH can end the drought, which means the drought-lament can only be addressed to YHWH. The final phrase you have made all these things grounds the hope in creation theology — the creator of weather controls weather.
